item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The assumptions and perspectives the texts provide are still kind of bleak. You touch upon some contexts, but do not develop them seriously. The observatory serves as a background for a commonplace discussion about very basic Hinduistic concepts (atman, reincarnation). Then, Callaghan demonstrates charmingly naïve сhauvinism (which suits the epoque and the character and really makes you think, but only reinforces already existing stereotypes about ignorant westerners, there is nothing really artsy or creative here). Other texts are descriptive in their nature and only convey the amazement of their author. Yes, we get it, the observatory is really special (we know very little why, though, which is deeply unfair to the reader).

I fully understand your opinion and respect it, but I cannot share it. I hope I didn't sound too harsh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Response
-Response

The problem with this critique, thoughtful though it is, is that the purpose of this article is not to puzzle the reader, though, or to create a mystery of any kind.

I did not want to create an article wherein the reader's question became what the scholars were trying to do at all- indeed, making the information given repetitive was part of the point. The different individuals who visit the observatory are seeing exactly the same thing, but because of the complete difference from culture to culture, create a whole series of assumptions and perspectives within their own minds to understand it. It becomes a kind of blank slate which they impose themselves onto.

If I were to do as you suggest, and transform each iteration so that it presented a substantially different theory as to the mystery of the observatory, then it'd shift the narrative focus away from where I want it to go. As it is, the framing of the article suggests that a mystery exists, and thus the reader is reading each piece with that in mind, but it should become gradually clear that no new information is being presented but only a shift in perspective. Cartwright's eventual realisation that the meaning doesn't matter is the point- that what the reader has experienced is a multiplicity of constructions of the Observatory, rather than any kind of mystery at all.

My point is thus: the article is not about the Observatory. It is about the people observing the Observatory. It is about the ways multiple people will see what is de facto the same thing but come to radically different conclusions based on the baggage they bring to the table. The observatory and its inhabitants have to be (substantially) identical and engaged in an identical purpose, or else the entire purpose of the article would be moot.

That the article is overly verbose and repetitive I don't deny (it's a perennial problem in my writing that I always struggle is), but I don't think that what you're suggesting would help illuminate the point of the article.

(Also thank you for the book recommendation and the note about Russian naming conventions, I will edit the latter in now :)).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a pity that this article is so repetitive, excessively verbose and abundant. My hopes were high, the choice of the subject was very to my liking, but now I feel disappointed and my expectations are frustrated.

In my opinion, the article doesn't employ the only stylistic device it should have. You have a bunch of historical texts, and I expect that each text should contain a different take on the story. They should contradict to each other, make the reader question their veracity, thus immersing them into the textual games. Milorad Pavić in Dictionary of the Khazars did this very thing profoundly well (and thematically it's somewhat close to your article, and it's really worth reading btw).

All your texts contain almost the same information with very few variations. In fact, the very first text already contains all the key details, so one may not read the others. And now let's imagine that each text narrates about a different topic:

  • the first one says that the observatory was used to explore the heavenly spheres to find God
  • the second one says that the scholars tried to figure out the exact date of the end of the world, using astrology

etc. etc. etc.

They can and should contradict to each other to puzzle the reader, and in the same time each one should add something to the big picture. The poor thing Cartwright in the end grieves that we will never be able to understand the true nature of the observatory. But the terrible thing is that you yourself, unfortunately, have spoilt the ending because you didn't give us the versions to choose from. It could have been an amazing article, but it's not. How sad! It's a downvote for me.

By the way, Katerina Karensky character should have been named correctly as Katerina Karenskaya. It should have the female ending in the surname. And her husband should have called her Katya, not so officially.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I completely agree- I was a little lazy with exactly how I wrote up each piece in terms of research, and I really did drop the ball on the Chinese section. I fully intend to alter that part at least, however; it might be a while but one day it will be altered to something more in line with how Chinese novels were written. Eventually :p.

I am glad you liked it overall, however, and good luck translating :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, the terrible net made me saving it again, and again…(Ignore me plz, really feel embarrassed, maybe it's a fault to write a reply in midnight).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, the terrible net made me saving it again, and again…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I like the idea… And I would apologize if the following words seem to be aggressive or there is other reasons that make you unpleasant, since my English is awful…

But still, the Chinese novel part made me feeling like I'm reading something like MacBeth, The Lord of the Rings or A Song of Ice and Fire or something alike about medieval European nobles… Because the mental activities part is too much I guess.

Most Chinese classical novels would advance the plots with long poems, many of which describe the scenes of landscape, historical scene spots, how the characters meet, fight, or even f**k (just imagine how popular raps are in modern-day pubs or popular cultures). Okay the poem-writing thing is much too difficult and if you ask me to write some classic poems I could barely say "yes" myself, but even without the poems, most plots are still concentrate more on scenes or dialogues than mental activities, which is much different from western novels, because early readers or listeners got the work to read some stories, so they would like to know what the interesting scenes rather than the long dull thoughts of the character, which is in fact something the philosopher would concentrate in while writing. This narrative tradition could still be seen in some of modern-day Wuxia or other Chinese-style novels written by Chinese.

I'm trying to translate that passage into Early Mandarin (17 c. right? That is the era of Early Mandarin). Still the work itself is great, bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a really cool read, and Cartwright's note did a great job at tying this all together. And no, I have zero bias regarding the SCP-3417 crosslink. Totally none. Totally.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hypothetically extant mountain" is one of those fantastic hooks you want to see at the start of an article, but my word, this does go on rather a while, doesn't it? I have to admit, I skipped from about the halfway point to the addendum, because it seemed like each historical note, while deep and well-written in turn, only revealed a small detail. It was a lot of work for not a lot of product, in other words.

But I like what the researcher at the end had to say, so +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially unsure whether this works conceptually as an SCP object, but reading it again I think it's a novel take on the format.

However, even if it made no sense as an SCP I'd still upvote, because it was an excellent read and the site is better for it being on the list. The way you can seamlessly and believably move between different writing styles across place and time is delightful.

+1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely superb. I've done a couple of research papers related to Eastern folklore in literature, and the verisimilitude here is downright spooky. And then at the end, it all ties together in a beautiful reflection on humankind's creative and academic relationship with myth. Holy crap. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fixed the link to SCP-2747 for you.

Also. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Turn off your mind, relax, and float down stream,
It is not dying, it is not dying.
Lay down all thoughts, surrender to the void,
It is shining, it is shining.
Yet you may see the meaning of within,
It is being, it is being.

With thanks to the following:

Kakroom
ModernMajorGeneral
Stallmantic
Gummy_Dragon
Tanhony
Weryllium

Credit to LSF for the name Li Yujian

You can find more of my stuff here.
